participants.reasoning: The extracted participants describe 373 enrolled children with multiple subgroup counts and age follow-up to 24 months, which does not match the official baseline total N of 227 (112/115) despite overlapping setting and age.
participants.extracted: Children enrolled in a randomized, controlled, double-blinded community-based trial in rural northern KwaZulu-Natal Province, South Africa, starting at age 6 months and followed until 24 months. A total of 373 infants/young children were enrolled between June 2003 and October 2004; 317 children had length-for-age data at two or more time points, including 25 HIV-infected children, 135 HIV-uninfected children born to HIV-infected mothers, and 157 HIV-uninfected children born to HIV-uninfected mothers. Baseline hemoglobin data were available for 254 children, and baseline plus 12-month hemoglobin data for 210 children. Children were excluded if they had received micronutrient supplements in the prior month, had severe undernutrition (weight below 60% of the NCHS median), nutritional edema, diarrhea lasting more than 7 days at enrollment, or were co-enrolled in other intervention trials.

Exclusion criteria: < 60% of median weight‐for‐age using United States NCHS standards; nutritional edema; received vitamin or micronutrient supplements in the previous month; diarrhea for > 7 d at the time of study enrollment; enrolled in another study of a clinical intervention

interventions.reasoning: The extracted text describes three regimens including a multiple-micronutrient-plus-zinc arm, whereas the official record only lists a zinc arm and a no-zinc placebo arm.
interventions.extracted: Children received one of three daily prophylactic supplement regimens from age 6 to 24 months: vitamin A alone (1250 IU; control), vitamin A plus zinc (1250 IU vitamin A plus 10 mg zinc as zinc gluconate), or multiple micronutrients plus zinc (1250 IU vitamin A plus 10 mg zinc and additional micronutrients including vitamins B1, B2, B6, B12, C, D, E, and K; copper; folate; iodine; iron; and niacin). Supplements were provided in identical blister packs and administered orally by caregivers by crushing the tablet into porridge; weekly home visits were used to support and monitor adherence.
interventions.official:
Group 1:zinc
Formulation: pill/tablet; compound: gluconate; frequency: daily; duration (months): 18; dose (mg): 10; co‐intervention(s): 1250 IU vitamin A
Group 2:no zinc
Placebo given; co‐intervention(s): 1250 IU vitamin A

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes emphasize growth measures (LAZ/weight-for-age) and mention diarrhea plus respiratory illness, but the official outcomes center on mortality, hospitalization, diarrhea subtypes, vomiting, hemoglobin, and anemia with specified time points, so the sets do not match.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure growth outcomes using longitudinal anthropometry, specifically change in length-for-age Z-score (LAZ) over 18 months from enrolment (6 months of age) to 24 months of age, assessed at scheduled clinic visits at 7, 8, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21, and 24 months. Length-for-age Z-scores and weight-for-age Z-scores were calculated using the WHO Child Growth Standards, with stunting defined as LAZ below -2. The study also planned to assess anemia as an exploratory outcome using hemoglobin concentration (g/dL) and anemia prevalence defined as hemoglobin below 11 g/dL, measured at baseline and again 12 months later. In addition, morbidity data on diarrhoea were collected weekly during home visits, with diarrhoea incidence/prevalence tracked over the intervention period; respiratory illness was part of the parent trial’s main morbidity aims, although the present report focused primarily on growth and anemia analyses.
